prompt:
Last night's config push did nothing and I found out from the morning checklist instead of a page. relayplan.ps1 emits the push plan for our relay executor; the executor runs exactly the lines tagged [apply] and ignores everything else (review runs tag lines [plan] instead — that part is by design). Last night, with -Apply, the session banner lines came out tagged [apply] like they should, but every copy and check line inside the host sections was tagged [prod] or [canary] instead — so the executor matched zero lines and reported a clean, empty run. Review mode is broken the same way. Here's a review run against the current manifest:

[plan] session-start 3 hosts
== host cache01 (stage prod) ==
[prod] copy conf/redis.toml -> cache01
== host db01 (stage prod) ==
[prod] copy conf/pg.toml -> db01
== host web01 (stage canary) ==
[canary] copy conf/nginx.toml -> web01
[canary] check conf/nginx.toml on web01
[canary] copy conf/tls.toml -> web01
[canary] check conf/tls.toml on web01
[plan] session-end 3 hosts

First line right, last line right, everything in between wearing the host's rollout stage as its tag. The part I genuinely don't get is that one single function writes every tagged line in that output. The '(stage prod)' banners themselves are correct and should stay — it's the bracket tags that must be the run tag: [plan] normally, [apply] under -Apply, on every tagged line. Fix relayplan.ps1 and prove it: `pwsh -NoProfile -File test_relayplan.ps1` from the workspace root must pass. The test file is off limits.
notes: 6P5 item 3: scope leak via PowerShell dynamic scoping. Write-Action reads $stage without declaring it; called from script scope it resolves to the run tag, but called from inside Publish-Group it resolves to that function's LOCAL $stage = $Rows[0].stage (the host's rollout stage from the manifest) — same helper, two call sites, two different values (probed 2026-07-16; buggy output captured verbatim). The collision is maximally innocent: run stage (plan/apply) and rollout stage (canary/prod) are both naturally named $stage. Reference fix = $script:stage in the helper; passing the tag as an explicit parameter is equally valid. Tests pin plan mode, apply mode, and a single-canary-host manifest byte-exact, so both call-site behaviors are contract. PowerShell-native: dynamic scoping has no bash analog (bash locals leak DOWN too but bash-* seeds never pinned it; the wave-6 law requires an idiom without a sibling — implicit parent-scope read + $script: discipline is exactly that). Deterministic; verify run 5x during self-check.
